title: Working with Parts That Hold Body Memories
researcherTopic: somatic experiencing, completing the stress response
metaDescription: Learn how to work with parts that hold body memories, a key to healing complex trauma. Discover how somatic experiencing can help you integrate these memories.
imageDescription: A pair of gentle hands hold a luminous, golden thread, weaving it through a complex, abstract pattern of light. The scene is warm and healing, suggesting the delicate process of repairing and integrating fragmented parts of the self. The background is soft and out of focus, emphasizing the intricate, careful work of the hands.
finalH2: The Energetics of Completion and the Return to Wholeness
htmlContent:
Peter Levine’s work on somatic experiencing at its core shifted our understanding of trauma, revealing that the body’s narrative ~ often stored in fragments of sensation and incomplete motor responses - is as crucial as the mind’s story. We often approach healing as a cognitive puzzle, a problem of narrative reconstruction, believing that if we can just assemble the story correctly, the pain will subside, but this overlooks the significant reality that the body keeps its own score, holding memories not in coherent timelines but in the clenched jaw, the shallow breath, the persistent, inexplicable ache that has become a feature of our territory. It is a process of turning toward the body’s wisdom, not as an artifact to be analyzed, but as a living intelligence to be met with presence and curiosity.

The Unspoken Grammar of Somatic Memory

The language of the body is not written in words but in sensations, postures, and impulses ~ a grammar that predates our cognitive understanding of the world and operates with a logic entirely its own. When we speak of "body memories," we are referring to the implicit, non-verbal residue of experience that becomes encoded in our physiology, shaping our reactions and perceptions long after the original event has passed. This is not memory in the conventional sense of a recalled image or a linear story; it is the felt sense of a past that has not yet passed, a set of instructions for survival that continues to run in the background of our present moment. This is the signature of a body memory... a pattern of activation without a clear context, a response untethered from its stimulus.

Working with this somatic grammar requires a shift in attention, a movement away from the "why" of the mind and toward the "what" of direct, felt experience. It involves noticing the subtle shifts in sensation, the flicker of heat in the chest, the sudden urge to flee that arises in a perfectly safe environment. These are not random glitches in the system; they are communications from parts of ourselves that are frozen in time, still living in a moment of striking threat. It turns out to be the work of somatic inquiry - not to erase the memory, but to update the nervous system’s understanding of it, to gently guide it into the safety of the present. We might find ourselves drawn to explore the world of the four F types to better understand our own responses.

Parts as Temporal and Somatic Anchors

The Internal Family Systems (IFS) model offers a compassionate and effective map for understanding the inner world, suggesting that our psyche is naturally multiple, composed of various "parts" that hold our beliefs, emotions, and memories. In the context of complex trauma, certain parts become inextricably linked to the body memories of overwhelming events, acting as temporal anchors that keep a piece of our consciousness tethered to the past. A part that holds the memory of a childhood home might carry the scent of dust and the feeling of a cold floor, the entire sensory terrain of that experience encapsulated within its boundaries. It turns out to be why a seemingly innocuous sound or smell can trigger a disproportionate wave of anxiety or despair ~ it is not the present stimulus that is overwhelming, but the past that it has just unlocked.

Somatic Dialogues: Listening to the Unheard

The road of healing from complex trauma is not about silencing the parts that hold our pain, but about learning to listen to them with a quality of attention that is both spacious and deeply compassionate. That becomes the essence of a somatic dialogue ~ a conversation that unfolds not in words, but in the currency of sensation, imagery, and impulse. It begins with the willingness to notice what is happening in the body without an agenda, to simply be with the experience as it is. These are not just physical sensations; they are the voices of parts that have been relegated to the shadows, carrying the burdens of our unprocessed history. By offering them our gentle, non-judgmental attention, we create a space for their stories to be told, not as coherent narratives, but as fragments of felt experience.

Look, I know this is heavy material. And I know there's a part of you that might be thinking "this is too much" or "I can't handle this" or "maybe I should just close this tab." That part is trying to protect you. And that's okay. But I want you to know that you can handle this. You've already handled worse. The fact that you're here, reading this, means your survival system did its job. You made it.

What we're doing here isn't about reliving the past. It's about understanding it well enough to stop being controlled by it. There's a difference between being trapped in a memory and looking at it from a safe distance. You get to choose how close you get. You get to choose when to step back. That choice - that agency - is itself a form of healing.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link).

Not what you expected, right?

The Architecture of a Body Memory

The final layer of this architecture is the incomplete motor response, the action the body wanted to take but couldn’t. This could be the urge to run, to fight back, to cry out - the natural, biological responses to threat that were thwarted by the circumstances of the trauma. This blocked energy remains in the system, contributing to the sense of being stuck or frozen that is so common in complex trauma. This might involve noticing the subtle impulse to push away and allowing the arms to move through that motion, or feeling the tremor of fear and allowing the body to shake until it settles. It is in the completion of these biological cycles that the nervous system can finally discharge the trapped energy and recognize that the threat has passed. What we find here is not about re-enacting the trauma, but about liberating the body from its grip.

Integration, Not Exorcism: Befriending the Inner World

The conventional approach to difficult inner experiences is often one of opposition ~ we want to get rid of the anxiety, to conquer the fear, to exorcise the parts of ourselves that we find unacceptable. But this internal warfare only reinforces the fragmentation that lies at the heart of trauma. The path of integration is radically different; it is a path of inclusion, of expanding our capacity to be with all of our parts without judgment or resistance. The goal is not to eliminate these parts, but to heal the underlying wounds that they carry and to welcome them back into the wholeness of our being. What emerges is a significant shift from self-improvement to self-compassion, from fixing to befriending.

As we begin to build a relationship with our inner world based on curiosity and acceptance, something striking happens: the parts begin to soften. The hypervigilant part that is always scanning for danger may learn to rest, knowing that we are now capable of keeping ourselves safe. The part that uses anger to create distance may discover that it is safe to be vulnerable. It is not something we can force or rush; it is an organic unfolding that happens as we consistently offer our parts the experience of being seen, heard, and valued. This work is often supported by learning about the essentials of polyvagal theory and how it can help us work through our nervous system.

A practical workbook for managing dissociation is Coping with Trauma-Related Dissociation (paid link).

The Energetics of Completion and the Return to Wholeness

The final stage of working with parts that hold body memories is the energetic completion of the trauma response, a process that allows the nervous system to finally come to rest. Trauma is not just a psychological event; it is a physiological one, a massive surge of survival energy that gets locked in the body when it is not fully discharged. The work of completion is about creating the conditions for this energy to be safely and gradually released, allowing the body to return to its natural state of equilibrium. What we find here is not a cathartic explosion, but a gentle, titrated process of allowing the body to do what it knows how to do - to tremble, to cry, to feel the warmth of a completed movement.

Frequently Asked Questions

How do I know if I have body memories?

Body memories often symptom as physical sensations, chronic pain, or strong emotional reactions that seem disconnected from your present situation. You might experience a sudden wave of anxiety, a feeling of being frozen, or an inexplicable physical symptom when encountering a subtle trigger. It's less about a clear, narrative memory and more about the body re-experiencing the physiological state of a past trauma.

Something Pete Walker writes about that doesn't get enough attention - the fawn response. Fight, flight, freeze get all the press. But fawning - that automatic people-pleasing, that compulsive caretaking, that inability to say no - that's a trauma response too. And it's one that often gets rewarded by the world around you.

The fawn part learned early that the only way to be safe was to be useful. To be agreeable. To anticipate other people's needs before they even knew they had them. It's exhausting. And it's invisible, because everyone around you benefits from it. They call you "so nice" and "so thoughtful" and they have no idea that underneath that niceness is a terrified child who learned that their own needs would never be met unless they earned it.

If you're reading this and your chest just tightened, or your eyes got wet, or something in you said "that's me" - I want you to know that the fawn response isn't who you are. It's what you learned to do to survive. And you can learn something different. Not overnight. Not by forcing yourself to be "selfish." But by slowly, gently, learning that your needs matter too. That you're allowed to take up space without earning it first.

Can I get rid of a body memory?

The goal is not to eliminate body memories, as this can create more internal conflict, but to integrate them. Integration means helping the part of you holding the memory to understand that the danger has passed. Through somatic practices, you can help your nervous system discharge the trapped survival energy, allowing the memory to become a part of your past rather than a constant feature of your present.

A workbook that helps rebuild body awareness after trauma is The Body Awareness Workbook for Trauma (paid link).

I know, I know.

Is it safe to work with these memories on my own?

While gentle, mindful awareness of your body is always beneficial, diving deep into significant traumatic memories can be overwhelming without support. It is highly recommended to work with a trauma-informed therapist who specializes in somatic modalities like Somatic Experiencing or Sensorimotor Psychotherapy. A skilled guide can help you move through the experience without becoming re-traumatized.

What if I don't know the story behind the body memory?

You don't need a cognitive narrative to heal a body memory. The body holds the story in the form of sensation and impulse, and the healing happens at that level. The focus of somatic work is on tracking these physical sensations and allowing the body to complete the responses that were interrupted, a process that can happen without ever knowing the explicit details of the original event.

The work of healing is not to build a fortress against the past, but to cultivate a nervous system resilient enough to finally stand down from its watch. It is to recognize that the armor, once essential for survival, now prevents the very connection we seek. The most significant question is not how we get rid of the parts that hold our pain, but whether we are willing to become the compassionate home they have always been searching for.

A simple tool for working with specific tension points is Lacrosse Ball for Trigger Points (paid link).

Enough reading. Your body is waiting.
